title: Military expert flags 'concrete proof' Scottish airport helps US bomb Iran

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: Glasgow Prestwick Airport is one of the airports used for military stopovers (Andrew Milligan/PA) A MILITARY intelligence expert has flagged “concrete proof” that a Scottish airport has been used to help the US bomb Iran. It comes after John Swinney said he would "consider" banning US military aircraft from using Prestwick Airport if it was confirmed they were involved in strikes on Iran.

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: Now, our analysis of flight data has revealed that US military aircraft departed Glasgow Prestwick Airport at least 32 times in the 10 days before the United States and Israel launched major strikes on Iran. On 21 of those occasions, it was to various key US and Nato military hubs in Europe and across the Middle East – and even onwards to Israel.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: READ MORE: Why do so many of Glasgow's historic buildings seem to end up in flames? US military planes have since continued to use Prestwick Airport almost every day as the war has escalated. When asked about these findings, Philip Ingram – a former army colonel and intelligence officer – said it was "concrete proof”.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – Military expert flags 'concrete proof' Scottish airport is helping US bomb Iran
